does: This paper proposes Trajectory Matching Policy Optimization (TMPO), a reinforcement learning method for aligning diffusion models that replaces scalar reward maximization with trajectory-level reward distribution matching. The core contribution is a Softmax Trajectory Balance (Softmax-TB) objective that matches policy probabilities over K sampled trajectories to a reward-induced Boltzmann distribution. The method theoretically inherits the mode-covering property of forward KL divergence, enabling it to preserve diversity across acceptable trajectories while still optimizing for high rewards. TMPO addresses the central problem of reward hacking in prior diffusion RL approaches, which suffer from mode collapse and unreliable reward signals by concentrating probability mass on few high-reward paths.
connects: This work builds directly on recent applications of RL to diffusion model alignment, extending prior methods that apply policy gradient and actor-critic approaches to guide image generation toward downstream task objectives. It addresses a known failure mode in these systems—reward hacking and mode collapse—that mirrors long-standing challenges in RL where scalar reward maximization leads to unintended optimization artifacts. The paper connects to the broader literature on divergence-based policy optimization and distributional RL, drawing on forward KL divergence properties and Boltzmann distributions from maximum entropy RL frameworks. The approach relates to trajectory-level reasoning in RL and builds on insights about mode-seeking versus mode-covering divergences used in variational inference and generative modeling.
enables: This work enables more robust RL-based alignment of diffusion models by reducing reward hacking while maintaining diversity, directly improving practical applicability of diffusion RL to tasks like image generation and editing. The trajectory-level matching framework could extend beyond diffusion models to other generative systems requiring RL alignment. The method opens research directions in understanding how to better balance reward optimization with distributional properties in policy learning. Future work could explore theoretical characterization of when trajectory-level constraints outperform scalar rewards, applications to other domains like language model alignment, and combinations with uncertainty quantification for more reliable reward functions. The approach also suggests new avenues for designing objectives that explicitly preserve behavioral diversity in other constrained optimization settings.
